Welcome back everyone, glad to have you. In this video I'm going to talk about optimizing and scaling your ads. We're going to cover starting out with new ads on a new account, KPIs, your key performance indicators, charts, and how you can view your KPIs on them. We're going to walk you through some suggestions for checking your ads and what to do when you do check them and then give you some ad management tips. First things first, these are guidelines for starting a new account. You already have lots of ads and data you may want to use as a basis for determining your numbers and scale. We recommend staying up-to-date with Facebook ad policies too. Change frequently and you should constantly create and test your own unique ads. What worked yesterday may not work tomorrow. But when you're starting out, we recommend creating up to six different ad sets. You want to make sure you have different creative, which is image and video, and different copy. You can do a mix. So a couple use this image with this text and a couple different image with that text. A good starting budget is going to be $10 per ad set per day. But the budget on ad sets depends on your market. Areas that are huge like New York, you're going to have to spend a little bit more to compete against the other people advertising as well. When you're creating new ads, Facebook needs about 8,000 impressions to learn a new ad. This can take three to seven days, but it does depend. You want to avoid making changes to the ads until it's no longer showing that it's learning. And then the more data, the faster and better your ads will learn. Your accounts take more time and require more monitoring. You'll have to invest more time in it as you get more data. You're going to see better results over time. Key performance indicators, APIs. You need to know what your key performance indicators are. Primary one you want to look for is your cost per lead or your cost per result. The phrasing just depends on which type of ad you're running. That's going to be the amount of the Facebook had to spend of your budget to get that lead or result for the course of however long you're looking at the ad. We also have some secondary key performance indicators. There's the click-through rate, which is the number of people that saw your ad and actually clicked on it. There are impressions or views and that's just how many eyeballs, how many screens that ad was displayed on, and then cost per click. How many clicks for dollars it was, so how many dollars I had to spend to get each individual click. Now those secondary KPIs are not as important as cost per lead or cost per result. You always want to take that main KPI into account when you're looking at your ads. If you're having trouble deciding which one's better, which one's doing worse, then those secondary KPIs will come into play. This is going to vary on all of your ad sets though, so after you get familiar with it, you're going to start realizing what works better for you. Now the most important feature of monitoring and maintaining Facebook ad account is going to be the charts. Always review the charts for active ad sets when you're checking your ads. You can move your mouse over an item and click view charts. You can also do that chart if you already have it open, so if you've already clicked on an ad set or an ad or a campaign on the left-hand side of the list where it lists all everything that's there, you can click view chart and open it up. Once you get the chart up, you're able to change the date at the top right, so you can do lifetime which is a lifetime of the ad, or you can narrow it down through one of the presets. You can also choose a specific date range using the calendars on the left. You click one date and then the second calendar you click another, like update, it's going to give you that ring. Performance is going to be the most important tab. That's the one that opens up first when you open up your charts. That's going to show you results specifically over time, and then if you move your mouse over each day, you can see what the results were for that day. That's going to show how many results or leads you received. It's going to show the cost per percentage, how many people have reached, the amount of the ad spend for that day, or overall depending on that time range. Demographics is going to show you how your ad performed across different ages and genders. I'm only going to see women on the left, men on the right, and then it goes age vertically. Placement shows the performance across different placements like Facebook or Instagram or Messenger. This is really useful when you're trying to figure out where your ads are doing the best. On both placements and demographics, you can change which KPIs are shown on those charts. You just click on the KPI up at the top and you can choose one from the list. Now we're going to go through checking your ads. It's important that you're checking your ads at least twice per day. We recommend once in the morning, once in the evening. That way you aren't dividing your focus across all of your ad sets and it gives you a chance to focus on one subset of your ads. That way you can give it your full attention. The first check of the day should be looking for losers. These are ads that are not performing. An ad is not generating leads or the cost per is higher than other ads. You may want to consider pausing that ad set. If it's just been a day, you don't need to do it right then, but if it's consistent or if it's spending a lot or just not performing anymore, you may want to consider pausing all together. When you pause an ad set, look into moving the budget to a new ad set or find a performing ad set. An ad set that's doing well, add the budget to that. Always look at the charts for the ads that are not performing well. You want to look at it over time, so you can look at different days and if you made any recent changes to those ads or your ad account, you can try to align it and see if maybe that was what caused the change. Also check the demographics and placements for things to exclude to increase the performance. When you're looking at the demographics, maybe you see that it's spending a lot to reach a lot of men or a lot of people in a certain age range, but it's not converting well. So it's a very high result or you're not making any cost per results or leads at all there. And you may want to consider changing the ad set to exclude them. The second check of the day is looking for winners. And this is when you're focusing on just ads that are performing well. Consider budget increases for ads that are doing a small increase if it's doing slightly well or increase it over time. You're looking to really scale the ad. Look at the amount that's spent over the last seven days and use as a guideline to set a new budget. And just like with losers, you want to check demographics and placements for things to exclude to increase the performance. If you see somewhere that's consistently not performing well, demographics or placements, you may want to edit your ad so it doesn't target there. It can spend the money where it's best to you. Here's some ad management tips that we found. It's often better to try a new ad set than increasing the budget on those that are not performing as well. But instead of trying to increase the budget on an ad that's doing poorly to get it to do better, often it's better to just pause that and create a new ad set altogether. This also applies to ads that are performing well before and then stop performing for whatever reason or just over time. You want to aim to stay below 24 ad sets. The more ad sets you have, the more your attention is going to be divided. The more time it really takes to focus on. You have too many ads, you can get that number blindness where you're looking through it all and it just all kind of blends together. And at that point, sometimes you may miss things that should stand out to you. Always want to have new copy and creative ready ahead of time. Performance can change very quickly. In situations that maybe an ad stops performing all of a sudden or you have a lot of competition in your market all of a sudden. So one ad type is not performing anymore. If you already have new copy and creative ready, ready to go ahead and create those new ads and you don't have that lag time where you're waiting for new copy and creative creating the ad and the learning process. Always try to have new ads ready to go past new ads, make small changes, mix it around that way you're staying ahead of the game. One tip to scale ads is to clone a well performing ad and change the targeting on that clone. So they have a well performing ad for men. The copy and creative isn't very specifically targeted for men, but that's how you have a targeted right now. Consider cloning that ad and then targeting women and excluding them or do it for an age range. Or if you have a very large market, you may want to change your location slightly. That way you can see if you can get that same kind of result from those same ad that's been performing. And then occasionally you want to go through and review your old ads to find ads that performed well for a while but stopped and then consider go ahead and reactivating them. Maybe remove some of the targeting restrictions on them so they're targeting a broader audience. That way you're getting a chance to recoup some of your losses and time that you've invested in the ad set that was doing well. And sometimes it could have just been from forces out of your control. Maybe it just wasn't placing well at that time. It was better to pause it, but when you presume it, it gives you a chance to have an ad running and scale up your ads and see if it can perform. One thing to remember with ad accounts and mention at the beginning is that they get data over time and the more data that your ad account has, the better Facebook can try to figure out who your target audience is because they want you to feel that ads are successful so that way you spend more money on ads and so that way they get more money, you get more leads. I hope you enjoyed this video. We had a lot of information there. If you have any questions or any questions always reach out to our support team. We're happy to help you troubleshoot issues. Try to find the best solution for you. Have a great rest of your day.
